input_candidates.truth_value: High back stresses in soft austenite and forward stresses in hard ferrite generate high stresses at boundaries; stress multiplication facilitates collective glide of dislocations on the same slip plane, promoting planar slip and edge dislocation multiplication.

input_candidates.truth_value: Heterogeneous deformation generates high stress at ferrite grain boundaries; austenite (24.3%) acts as soft phase, effectively alleviating stress localization in ferrite and enabling deformation to higher stresses.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Back stresses generated in soft austenite and forward stresses in hard ferrite during deformation of heterogeneous structure.

input_candidates.truth_value: The 8Al steel exhibits a five-stage strain hardening behavior (stages A-E), closely resembling that of high-carbon high-aluminum austenitic lightweight steels, and is attributed to the dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) mechanism in ferrite.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Densities measured by Archimedes principle: 4Al = 7.42 g·cm⁻³, 6Al = 7.18 g·cm⁻³, 8Al = 6.99 g·cm⁻³, 10Al = 6.84 g·cm⁻³.

input_candidates.truth_value: Slip band spacing in ferrite of 8Al steel refines from 780 nm at 0% engineering strain to 300 nm at 10%, 120 nm at 20%, and 80 nm at 28% strain. Austenite dislocation cell block size refines from 900 nm to 240 nm, and dislocation cell size from 280 nm to 220 nm.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Calculated stacking fault energies of austenite at room temperature (Table 2): 4Al_γ = 51.25 mJ·m⁻², 6Al_γ = 62.64 mJ·m⁻², 8Al_γ = 68.47 mJ·m⁻².

input_candidates.truth_value: In 10Al steel, the coarsened DO3 ordered domains (~19 nm) cannot be fully sheared; only a few long straight screw dislocations are activated, leading to brittle fracture. Ductility is also severely degraded by 18R martensite precipitates.
input_candidates.prediction_value: DO3 ordered domain sizes in ferrite from TEM dark-field images: ~2.0 nm in 6Al, ~2.1 nm in 8Al, and ~19 nm in 10Al; domain size increases with Al content.

input_candidates.truth_value: The number of dislocation pairs (up to 6 in 10Al, up to 3 in 8Al) corresponds to the DO3 domain size; using D = N b with b=0.25 nm gives calculated domain sizes of 1.5 nm for 8Al (close to TEM-observed 2.1 nm) and 3.0 nm for 10Al (much smaller than TEM 19 nm, indicating large domains are incompletely sheared).
input_candidates.prediction_value: 18R martensite acicular precipitates observed in 10Al ferrite after quenching are significantly enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al; this martensite is identified as a ductility-reducing phase.

input_candidates.truth_value: In 4Al steel, the similitude coefficients K_SB+DCB = 2.03 and K_DC = 2.02 derived from the slip band/dislocation cell refinement relation closely match values reported for austenitic stainless steels (2.0–2.9).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dislocation pairing in ferrite due to APB energy: up to 3 pairs in 8Al and up to 6 pairs with ~3 nm spacing in 10Al. Estimated ordered domain sizes from pairing: ~1.5 nm (8Al) and ~3.0 nm (10Al), with 8Al value close to TEM observation (~2.1 nm) but 10Al value much smaller than TEM observation (~19 nm), indicating 10Al domains are difficult to completely shear.

input_candidates.truth_value: Microhardness of austenite in 4Al and 8Al increases monotonically with strain, while ferrite in 8Al initially softens then hardens, indicating differences in deformation mechanisms between the two phases.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Uniform elongation values from Table 3: 4Al = 38.5%, 6Al = 37.6%, 8Al = 31.0%, 10Al = 2.1%.

input_candidates.truth_value: The short-range ordered L12 structure in austenite was detected by satellite diffraction spots near the [100]γ zone axis; its intensity decreases with increasing nominal Al content.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Microhardness evolution with true strain (Fig. 3e): 8Al ferrite initially softens then hardens, while austenite hardness increases monotonically; 4Al austenite hardens monotonically.

input_candidates.truth_value: Acicular 18R martensite in 10Al steel is significantly enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al, as revealed by TEM EDS mapping.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Strengthening contributions to 8Al flow stress (Table 4): at 3.0% engineering strain, σ_(α)=41 MPa and σ_(γ)=28 MPa; at 28.0% engineering strain (24.7% true strain, true stress 924 MPa), σ_(α)=347 MPa and σ_(γ)=111 MPa.

input_candidates.truth_value: The stacking fault energy of austenite increases with Al content from 51.25 mJ/m² (4Al) to 68.47 mJ/m² (8Al) at room temperature.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Average slip band spacing in ferrite (α_SB), dislocation cell block size (γ_SB+DCB), and dislocation cell size (γ_DC) for 8Al at engineering strains 0%, 10%, 20%, 28% documented in Fig. 6: e.g., α_SB decreases from 780 nm to 80 nm.

input_candidates.truth_value: The densities of investigated steels are 7.42, 7.18, 6.99, and 6.84 g/cm³ for 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al, respectively, measured by Archimedes principle.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Satellite diffraction spots in austenite SADPs (Fig. 1e-h) indicate spinodal decomposition and L1_2 short-range ordering of Al and C; intensity decreases with increasing nominal Al content.

candidates.truth_value: The 8Al steel exhibits a five-stage strain hardening behavior (stages A-E), closely resembling that of high-carbon high-aluminum austenitic lightweight steels, and is attributed to the dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) mechanism in ferrite.

candidates.truth_value: Slip band spacing in ferrite of 8Al steel refines from 780 nm at 0% engineering strain to 300 nm at 10%, 120 nm at 20%, and 80 nm at 28% strain. Austenite dislocation cell block size refines from 900 nm to 240 nm, and dislocation cell size from 280 nm to 220 nm.

candidates.truth_value: In 10Al steel, the coarsened DO3 ordered domains (~19 nm) cannot be fully sheared; only a few long straight screw dislocations are activated, leading to brittle fracture. Ductility is also severely degraded by 18R martensite precipitates.
candidates.prediction_value: DO3 ordered domain sizes in ferrite from TEM dark-field images: ~2.0 nm in 6Al, ~2.1 nm in 8Al, and ~19 nm in 10Al; domain size increases with Al content.

candidates.truth_value: The number of dislocation pairs (up to 6 in 10Al, up to 3 in 8Al) corresponds to the DO3 domain size; using D = N b with b=0.25 nm gives calculated domain sizes of 1.5 nm for 8Al (close to TEM-observed 2.1 nm) and 3.0 nm for 10Al (much smaller than TEM 19 nm, indicating large domains are incompletely sheared).
candidates.prediction_value: 18R martensite acicular precipitates observed in 10Al ferrite after quenching are significantly enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al; this martensite is identified as a ductility-reducing phase.
